content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 20, 2007

Michael Prandini
Treasurer
Citizens for Housing Opportunities
7108 North Fresno Street
Suite 305
Fresno, CA 93720

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-218

Dear Mr. Prandini:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question is
general in nature, we provide you with informal assistance.² Please note that the Fair
Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice;
this advice is based solely on the facts that you provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May a political action committee ("PAC") contribute more than $250 to a
LAFCO commission member subject to the provisions of Section 84308, without
triggering the prohibition and disclosure rules under Section 84308, when members of the
PAC's board have applications before the LAFCO?

CONCLUSION

So long as the contributions by the PAC are not directed and controlled by the
same individual, or majority of the same individuals, involved in the application before
the LAFCO, the PAC may contribute more than $250 to any LAFCO commission
member without triggering the prohibition and disclosure rules under Section 84308.

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109 – 18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a commission opinion or formal
written advice. (See reg. 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-06-218
Page No. 2


FACTS

You are the treasurer of a political action committee called Citizens for Housing Opportunities ("CHO-PAC"). CHO-PAC supports the Building Industry Association of Fresno/Madera Counties (the "Association"). CHO-PAC receives all of its funding from Association members.

CHO-PAC has at least four board members. The four CHO-PAC board members you mention are also board members and builder-members of the Association.

You indicate that members of the CHO-PAC board "have had, and may have in the future, applications before" the Fresno County Local Agency Formation Commission ("Fresno LAFCO"). You do not provide facts regarding the nature of any specific "applications" that may come before the Fresno LAFCO, whether the four board members constitute a majority of the CHO-PAC board, or whether the various members of the board co-own business entities with applications before the Fresno LAFCO. Additionally, you do not indicate whether the CHO-PAC board members mentioned would be presenting applications to the Fresno LAFCO jointly, individually, as agents for others, and/or through the auspices of a business entity or entities.


ANALYSIS

A. The General Application of Section 84308.

In 1983, Section 84308 was added to the Act in order to ensure that appointed members of boards or commissions would not be biased by large campaign contributors or potential contributors who might appear before them in a proceeding involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use.

1. Persons & Agencies To Which Section 84308 Applies.

Generally, Section 84308 applies to all elected or appointed officers (including alternates and candidates for elective office) of state or local government agencies. Section 84308(a)(3) & (a)(4).) Section 84308 does not apply to the courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government, local government agencies whose members are directly elected by the voters, the Legislature, the Board of Equalization, or constitutional officers. (Section 84308(a)(3).) However, Section 84308 does apply to persons who are members of an exempted agency, but are acting as voting members of another agency. (Ibid.)

Unless Fresno LAFCO comprises members that are directly elected by the voters to the Fresno LAFCO board, Section 84308 will apply to the agency.

File No. I-06-218
Page No. 3

2. Disqualifications, Disclosures and Prohibitions Triggered When Persons
Who Have a License, Permit, or Other Entitlement for Use Pending Before An
Agency Contribute Over $250 to an Agency Officer.

The First Prohibition. Section 84308 states that "no officer of an agency shall
accept, solicit, or direct a contribution of more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250),
from any party, or his or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent, while a
proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is pending before the
agency and for three months following the date a final decision is rendered in the
proceeding if the officer knows or has reason to know that the participant has a financial
interest . . . ." (Section 84308(b); Reg. 18438.7 [discussing prohibitions and
disqualifications]; see Section 84308(d) [describing how similar prohibitions are enforced
against parties, participants, and their agents]; also see description of what constitutes a
"financial interest" in Section 87103 and Sections 87100 et seq.)

A "party" is "any person who files an application for, or is the subject of, a
proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use." (Section
84308(a)(1).) A "participant" is:

"any person who is not a party but who actively supports or opposes a
particular decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other
entitlement for use and who has a financial interest in the decision . . . .
A person actively supports or opposes a particular decision in a
proceeding if he or she lobbies in person the officers or employees of the
agency, testifies in person before the agency, or otherwise acts to
influence officers of the agency."

(Section 84308(a)(2); see also Reg. 18438.4 [defining what constitutes
lobbying, testifying and acting to influence].)

Regulation 18438.3 states that, for purposes of Section 84308, "a person is
the 'agent' of a party to, or a participant in, a proceeding involving a license, permit
or other entitlement for use only if he or she represents that person in connection
with the proceeding involving the license, permit or other entitlement for use."
(Regulation 18438.3(a).) Activities including calling, writing a letter to, or
testifying before a member of a governing board can create an agency relationship
for purposes of Section 84308. (Petzold Advice Letter, No. A-03-094; Steiner
Advice Letter, No. A-02-195.) Whether other activities create an agency
relationship in this context involves a fact-sensitive analysis that is not for the
Commission to decide. (Petzold Advice Letter, No. A-03-094.)

Depending upon the nature of the specific proceeding being analyzed, the
proceeding may or may not involve "a license, permit, or other entitlement for use" under
Section 84308. (See Reg. 18438.2; City of Agoura Hills v. Local Agency Formation

File No. I-06-218
Page No. 4

Comm. (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 480, 497-499 [holding that certain "sphere of influence"
proceedings before a LAFCO did not come within the scope of Section 84308]; see also
Alsop Advice Letter, No. A-04-079 [describing types of decisions which come within the
ambit of Section 84308].) For purposes of this letter we will assume that the
"applications" you refer to come within the scope of Section 84308 as a license, permit,
or other entitlement for use.

Disclosure Obligations and Other Prohibitions. If an officer of an agency
receives a contribution exceeding $250 from a party or participant within the 12 months
prior to rendering a decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other
entitlement for use, the officer must disclose that fact on the record of the proceeding.
(Section 84308(c); see 84308(d) [describing how similar disclosure obligations are placed
upon a "party" and his or her agents].) Moreover, if an officer has willfully or knowingly
received such a contribution during that time period from a party or his or her agent, or
from a participant, or his or her agent if the officer knows or has reason to know that the
participant has a "financial interest" in the decision, the officer cannot make, participate
in making, or in any way attempt to use his or her official position to influence the
decision. (Section 84308(c).) This last prohibition will not apply if the officer returns
the contribution within 30 days from the time he or she knows, or should have known,
about the contribution and the proceeding. (Section 84308(c), last paragraph.)

A PAC can be a party or participant (or agent thereof) under Section 84308.
Whether a contribution exceeding $250 by a PAC triggers application of Section 84308
to a person or entity that contributes to (or is a member of) the PAC depends upon
whether the person or entity "directs and controls" the PAC, which is discussed below.
(See Sutton Advice Letter, No. A-95-156 [regarding which persons or entities associated
with a PAC will trigger the disqualification of an official due to the PAC], copy enclosed.)

B. When Contributions From Different Sources Are Aggregated Under Section
84308.

The Act provides two rules that address when contributions are aggregated. The
Commission has long advised that if one individual, or a majority of the same
individuals, "directs and controls" the contributions of two or more persons,³ then the
contributions of all will be aggregated. (Stergakos Advice Letter, No. I-04-149; see, e.g.,
Breckenridge Advice Letter, No. I-94-126.) This rule was developed from "two 1976
Commission Opinions, In re Lumsdon 2 FPPC Ops. 140, and In re Kahn 2 FPPC Ops.
151 . . . which generally set out the Commission's views on the aggregation of
contributions." (Stergakos Advice Letter, No. I-04-149.) The basic aggregation rule, as
developed from Lumsdon and Kahn is currently set forth in Regulation 18215.1.

³ Section 82047 defines "person" under the Act as "an individual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company,
association, committee, and any other organization or group of persons acting in concert."

File No. I-06-218
Page No. 5


That regulation states:

"§ 18215.1. Contributions; When Aggregated.

"(a) Definitions. For purposes of determining when contributions are
aggregated under the provisions of this title:

"(1) "Entity" means any person, other than an individual;

"(2) "Majority owned" means an ownership of more than fifty percent.

"(b) The contributions of an entity whose contributions are directed and
controlled by any individual shall be aggregated with contributions made
by that individual and any other entity whose contributions are directed
and controlled by the same individual;

"(c) If two or more entities make contributions that are directed and
controlled by a majority of the same persons, the contributions of those
entities shall be aggregated;

"(d) Contributions made by entities that are majority owned by any
individual shall be aggregated with the contributions of the majority owner
and all other entities majority owned by that person, unless those entities
act independently in their decisions to make contributions.

"Comment: See section 18428 regarding aggregation of contributions
under Chapter 4 and Chapter 5 of this title. Also see In re Lumsdon
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 140 and In re Kahn (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 151."

Applying the Commission's longstanding approach to the aggregation of
contributions, we are guided by the two following principles. First, if the decision by one
or more entities to make contributions is directed and controlled by one individual, we
must aggregate the contributions made by the individual and the entities the individual
controls. Second, if the decision by one or more entities to make contributions is directed
and controlled by more than one individual, aggregation is generally not required unless
there is a majority of the same individuals directing and controlling the contributions from
those entities. (See generally, Farley Advice Letter, No. A-89-628; Pines Advice Letter,
No. I-89-703; Grant Advice Letter, No. I-90-144; Olson Advice Letter, A-90-302; Boyle
Advice Letter, No. A-90-120; Rossi Advice Letter, No. I-93-211; Bagatelos Advice
Letter, No. I-90-457; Sutton Advice Letter, No. A-95-156.)⁴

⁴ It should be noted that pursuant to the second principle, less than a majority of persons may still
be deemed to control the contribution decisions of an entity. In the Fechner Advice Letter (I-03-263), the

File No. I-06-218
Page No. 6


In addition to Regulation 18215.1, there are separate rules applicable to
proceedings under Section 84308. Your facts indicate that the LAFCO commission
members are subject to the contribution limits of Section 84308.

Regulation 18438.5, which became operative in June 2006 provides an additional
factor to be used in determining aggregation of contributions under Section 84308:

"For purposes of Government Code section 84308: [¶] Notwithstanding
the provisions of 2 Cal. Code Regs. section 18215.1, to determine
whether a contribution of more than $250 has been made by any party to
a proceeding, contributions made by a party's parent, subsidiary, or
otherwise related business entity, (as those relationships are defined in 2
Cal. Code of Regs. section 18703.1(d)) shall be aggregated and treated
as if received from the party for purposes of the limitations and
disclosure provisions of Government Code section 84308." (Reg.
18438.5.)

Therefore, unless CHO-PAC is an "otherwise related business entity," the
provisions of Regulation 18438.5 do not apply. Since an "otherwise related business
entity" (defined in Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)) must be a for-profit organization or
enterprise, and since CHO-PAC is probably not a for-profit enterprise, Regulation
18438.5 probably does not apply to CHO-PAC.

Accordingly, as long as contributions made by CHO-PAC to a Fresno LAFCO
commission member are not directed and controlled by the same individual, or a majority
of the same individuals, that have an application for a license, permit or other entitlement
for use before the LAFCO, CHO-PAC may contribute more than $250 to a LAFCO
member, or his or her controlled committee, and not subject the described individual or
majority of individuals, to the prohibition and disclosure rules under Section 84308.




requestor was advised that a company president who owned the company in equal shares with his three
sisters (i.e., less than a majority of shares) was required to aggregate his contributions with those of the
company because he was the only sibling who was active in the management of the company.

File No. I-06-218
Page No. 7


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Andreas C. Rockas
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ACR:jgl

You do not provide facts regarding the nature of any specific "applications" that may come before the Fresno LAFCO, whether the four board members constitute a majority of the CHO-PAC board, or whether the various members of the board co-own business entities with applications before the Fresno LAFCO. ANALYSIS A. The General Application of Section 84308. In 1983, Section 84308 was added to the Act in order to ensure that appointed members of boards or commissions would not be biased by large campaign contributors or potential contributors who might appear before them in a proceeding involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use. 1.
